title: fix: tests webserver syntaxwarning
body:
> Changed in Python version 3.12: Unrecognized escape sequences produce a SyntaxWarning. In a future Python version they will be eventually a SyntaxError.
[2.4.1.1. Escape sequences](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/lexical_analysis.html#index-23)

Change to raw string literal to fix:
```
/webserver.py:17: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\d'
  elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path):
/webserver.py:18: SyntaxWarning: invalid escape sequence '\d'
  result = re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)
```

files_modified.patch:
@@ -14,8 +14,8 @@ def do_GET(self):
             response_body += self.headers.as_string()
         elif self.path == "/port":
             response_body += f"answer from port {PORT}\n"
-        elif re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path):
-            result = re.match("/status/(\d+)", self.path)
+        elif re.match(r"/status/(\d+)", self.path):
+            result = re.match(r"/status/(\d+)", self.path)
             response_code = int(result.group(1))
             response_body += f"answer with response code {response_code}\n"
         elif self.path == "/":

steps.operation: Fix invalid escape sequence warnings by converting the regex pattern string to a raw string literal in the GET request handler
steps.reason: Python 3.12 emits a SyntaxWarning for unrecognized escape sequences like `\d` in normal string literals; using a raw string literal (`r"..."`) preserves the intended regex and avoids future incompatibility where this may become a SyntaxError.
steps.side_effects: Only affects request-path matching for the `/status/<code>` route in this test webserver; behavior should remain the same, but any code or tests relying on the previous (warning-producing) literal form will now run cleanly under Python 3.12+.

step_plan.summary: Updates the test webserver’s `/status/<code>` regex to use raw string literals, eliminating Python 3.12 invalid escape sequence SyntaxWarnings while preserving matching behavior.
